graph.confusable_reason: Both produce mid-to-late-season wilt/yellowing with dead plants in patchy clusters along the row, white mycelium associated with the lower stem/root, and dark-pigmented fungal structures, so a field scout seeing wilting plants with internal white fungal growth can confuse them. They are separated cleanly by WHERE the fungus expresses itself (external soil-line mat with mustard-seed sclerotia for southern blight vs internal black-stained taproot with cortical stroma for taproot decline).
graph.shared_presentation: Scattered wilting, yellowing, and dying soybean plants appearing in patches within a row, often from mid-season onward, with white fungal mycelium found at or below the soil line and dark/blackened fungal structures on or in the lower stem and roots. Both are warm-climate, southern-US soilborne fungal diseases that can kill plants outright.

forks.question: At what growth stage do plants first collapse?
forks.a_signal: Can strike at any stage including seedling damping-off (pre/post-emergence) as well as mature plants
forks.b_signal: Foliar collapse concentrated at reproductive pod-fill (R4-R6), though seedlings can occasionally show it

forks.question: How do the leaves die and stay on the plant?
forks.a_signal: Whole plant yellows and wilts; leaves turn brown and remain attached to the dead stem
forks.b_signal: Distinct interveinal chlorosis and necrosis (yellow/brown between veins) preceding plant death

forks.question: Where is the lesion and fungal activity located relative to the soil surface?
forks.a_signal: Dark brown girdling lesion exactly at the soil line, with fungal activity external on the stem base and surrounding soil/residue
forks.b_signal: Damage is internal and below ground in the taproot and lateral roots; plants snap off at the soil line leaving rotted roots behind

forks.question: What does the EXTERNAL stem base / soil surface look like for signs?
forks.a_signal: Conspicuous white fan-like mycelial mats on stem base, residue and soil, studded with round mustard-seed-sized sclerotia (tan to reddish-brown to dark brown)
forks.b_signal: No surface mat or mustard-seed sclerotia; instead black stromatic crusts on roots and occasional black-tipped 'dead man's fingers' stromata on roots/debris

forks.question: Split the lower stem/taproot longitudinally and examine the root surface and pith.
forks.a_signal: Taproot interior largely normal; signs are EXTERNAL (mat plus sclerotia) at the crown; no charcoal-black staining of the taproot vascular/cortical tissue
forks.b_signal: Taproot surface shows darkened charcoal-black thin stromatic tissue with white cottony mycelium inside the pith/cortex when split

forks.question: What weather and soil conditions accompanied the outbreak?
forks.a_signal: Outbreak tied to hot (77-95F) humid spells; thrives on residue at the soil surface near warm, moist crowns
forks.b_signal: Associated with continuous soybean/reduced rotation in southern soils; less tightly tied to a specific heat-humidity trigger

graph.narrative_a: Southern blight (Athelia/Sclerotium rolfsii) is a warm-weather soilborne fungus with a very wide host range that attacks the soybean crown at the soil line. Plants yellow, wilt, and die with brown leaves clinging to a girdled stem. The diagnostic giveaway is EXTERNAL: a white fan-like mycelial mat blanketing the stem base, residue, and soil, dotted with numerous round sclerotia the size of mustard seeds that mature from tan to reddish-brown to dark brown. Splitting the taproot reveals essentially normal internal tissue, confirming the pathogen lives on the outside of the crown rather than staining the root core.
graph.narrative_b: Taproot decline (Xylaria necrophora; reported as Xylaria sp./arbuscula) is an emerging southern-US root pathogen. Foliar interveinal chlorosis and necrosis typically appear at pod fill (R4-R6), and affected plants snap off easily at the soil line because the rot is concentrated in the taproot below ground. The diagnostic giveaway is INTERNAL and rooted: charcoal-black thin stromatic tissue coating the taproot and lateral roots, white cottony mycelium inside the pith when the root is split, and sometimes black-tipped 'dead man's fingers' stromata on roots and crop debris. There is no soil-line mat of mustard-seed sclerotia.
graph.management_a: Reduce inoculum and crown wetness: bury crop residue with deep tillage to bury sclerotia (they survive 3-4 years near the surface), rotate to nonhost grasses/corn out of the broad host range, avoid dense canopies and excessive surface debris around crowns, and improve drainage. Fungicides have limited utility once mats form; focus on cultural prevention and seed treatments where seedling damping-off is a risk.
graph.management_b: Manage through rotation and genetics: rotate away from continuous soybean to nonhost crops (e.g., corn) to break the soilborne stroma reservoir, manage crop residue/debris that harbors stromata, plant the least-susceptible available varieties, and avoid moving the pathogen on equipment/soil. No highly effective in-season fungicide rescue exists; emphasis is on rotation and reducing infested debris.

lay.farmer_lay_report: I've got soybeans wilting, yellowing, and dying off in patches down the rows, mostly showing up from mid-season on. Down near the soil line I can see white fuzzy growth on the lower stems and roots, with some dark, blackened crusty stuff on the lower part of the plant.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Dig up a dying plant and split the main taproot open: inside it looks normal-colored and healthy, not stained dark. The trouble is all on the OUTSIDE at the crown right at the soil line, where you'll see a white fan-like fuzzy mat and tiny round tan-to-brown balls about the size of mustard seeds clinging to the stem base.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Pull a dying plant and look at the main taproot: the outside surface has thin charcoal-black crusty patches. Split that taproot lengthwise and the center pith/inner wood is packed with white cottony growth.
